title: Unauthorized Firmware Upload Attempt on RTU

compliance.assurance_rationale: Uplifted from contributing to partial: UC provides meaningful monitoring evidence for CR 2.1.
compliance.controlObjective: Evidence that IEC 62443 CR 2.1 is enforced — Splunk UC-22.15.37: Unauthorized Firmware Upload Attempt on RTU.
compliance.evidenceArtifact: Saved search 'UC-22.15.37' running on claroty:alert category=firmware; pan:traffic ftp/scp, archived to the restricted audit_evidence index (default 7-year retention). Auto-drafted — SME review required.

spl:
index=ot_sec sourcetype="claroty:alert" category="firmware" earliest=-14d
| append [ search index=fw sourcetype="pan:traffic" app IN ("ftp","scp","sftp") dest_zone="OT-RTU" earliest=-14d
    | lookup ot_engineering_nets.csv src OUTPUT role | where role!="ews" ]
| stats earliest(_time) as t0, values(src) as sources by dest, signature, sourcetype
| sort - t0
description: Detects firmware or OS image transfer sessions to RTUs from non-engineering hosts, testing authorization enforcement on components.
value: Gives the responsible team auditor-ready evidence for IEC 62443 compliance by automating regulatory compliance evidence using indexes fw, ot_sec and sourcetypes claroty:alert, pan:traffic. Replaces manual evidence gathering with a continuous Splunk search that produces structured, time-stamped artefacts suitable for regulatory examination and internal audit review.
implementation: (1) Block firmware protocols except via jump host; (2) require signed images; (3) alert any ftp to field; (4) integrate vendor secure boot logs if available. Indexes required: `index=fw`, `index=ot_sec`; Sourcetypes: `sourcetype=claroty:alert`, `sourcetype=pan:traffic`; Lookups: `ot_engineering_nets`, `ot_engineering_nets.csv`.
visualization: Timeline, Table, Single value (events). Key fields: `dest`, `signature`, `sources`, `sourcetype`, `t0`.
knownFalsePositives: Zone communications during authorized engineering, vendor maintenance, or planned commissioning.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Dragos/Claroty alerts + firewall.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `claroty:alert` category=firmware; `pan:traffic` ftp/scp.
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
(1) Block firmware protocols except via jump host; (2) require signed images; (3) alert any ftp to field; (4) integrate vendor secure boot logs if available.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=ot_sec sourcetype="claroty:alert" category="firmware" earliest=-14d
| append [ search index=fw sourcetype="pan:traffic" app IN ("ftp","scp","sftp") dest_zone="OT-RTU" earliest=-14d
    | lookup ot_engineering_nets.csv src OUTPUT role | where role!="ews" ]
| stats earliest(_time) as t0, values(src) as sources by dest, signature, sourcetype
| sort - t0
```

Understanding this SPL

**Unauthorized Firmware Upload Attempt on RTU (IEC 62443-4-2 / CR 2.1)** — Detects firmware or OS image transfer sessions to RTUs from non-engineering hosts, testing authorization enforcement on components.

Documented **Data sources**: `claroty:alert` category=firmware; `pan:traffic` ftp/scp. **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Dragos/Claroty alerts + firewall. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: ot_sec; **sourcetype**: claroty:alert. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=ot_sec, sourcetype="claroty:alert", time bounds. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• Appends rows from a subsearch with `append`.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by dest, signature, sourcetype** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions.
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.

Step 3 — Validate
Compare with the ESP or perimeter firewall log (for example Palo Alto or FortiGate) for the same window. Spot-check a few source events, reconcile counts, and verify field extractions, thresholds, and index permissions for the people who need this evidence.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Timeline, Table, Single value (events).
grandmaExplanation: We detects firmware or OS image transfer sessions to RTUs from non-engineering hosts, testing authorization enforcement on components. That helps protect factories, plants, and the gear that runs them from mistakes and bad actors the standards call out.
